chapter.p: Waking near noon that day from the sleep into which she had fallen after the vigil on Thundertop, H√©lo√Øse Spear sat up in bed and thought: ‚ÄúWhat‚Äôs wrong?‚Äù

She stretched her arms above her head, pushed back her hair from her sleepy eyes, and looked about her room, which was cooled by the soft green light filtered through the leaves of the ancient trumpet creeper above her window. For a moment or two the present was drowned in the remembered blaze of the sunrise, and the enjoyment of the hunger satisfied beside the pool; but already she knew that‚ÅÝ‚Äîas was almost invariable in her experience‚ÅÝ‚Äîsomething disagreeable lurked in the heart of her memories: as if every enjoyment in life had to be bought by a bother.

‚ÄúThat boy has eyes‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI was right,‚Äù she thought; and then, immediately: ‚ÄúOh, I know, the motor!‚Äù For she had remembered, in the act of re-evoking young Weston, that on the way home the car had stopped suddenly, a mile or more above Eaglewood, and that, after a long struggle (during which he had stood helplessly watching her, seemingly without an idea as to how he might come to her aid) they had had to abandon it by the roadside, and she had parted with her companion at the Eaglewood gates after telling him how to find his way home on foot. She herself had meant to take a couple of hours of sleep, and then slip out early to catch the man-of-all-work before the household was up, and persuade him to get the delinquent motor home somehow. But as soon as she had undressed, and thrown herself on her bed, she sank into the bottomless sleep of youth; and here it was nearly lunchtime, and everybody downstairs, and the absence of the motor doubtless already discovered! And, with matters still undecided between herself and Lewis Tarrant, she had not especially wanted him to know that she had been out without him to see the sunrise‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe more so as he would never believe she had gone up to Thundertop unaccompanied.

Ah, how she envied the girls of her age who had their own cars, who led their own lives, sometimes even had their own bachelor flats in New York! Except as a means to independence riches were nothing to her; and to acquire them by marriage, and then coldly make use of them for her own purposes, was as distasteful to her as anything in her present life. And yet she longed for freedom, and saw no other way to it. If only her eager interest in life had been matched by some creative talent! She could half paint, she could half write‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut her real gift (and she knew it) was for appreciating the gifts of others. Even had discipline and industry fostered her slender talents they would hardly have brought her a living. She had measured herself and knew it‚ÅÝ‚Äîand what else was there for her but marriage? ‚ÄúOh, well,‚Äù she thought, for the thousandth time, ‚Äúsomething may turn up‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù which meant, as she knew, that her mother‚Äôs cousin, old Tom Lorburn, might drop off at any moment and leave her the Willows, with enough money to get away from it, and from Paul‚Äôs Landing, forever.
She heard her mother‚Äôs fluttering knock, and Mrs. Spear came in, drooping, distressed, and dimly beautiful.

‚ÄúOh, Halo‚ÅÝ‚Äîasleep still? I‚Äôm sorry to disturb you, darling, but it‚Äôs after eleven‚Äù (‚ÄúI know, I know,‚Äù grumbled H√©lo√Øse, always impatient of the obvious), ‚Äúand something so tiresome has happened. The motor has disappeared. Jacob says somebody must have got into the garage in the night. The cook says she saw a dreadful-looking man hanging about yesterday evening; but the trouble is that the garage lock wasn‚Äôt broken open‚ÅÝ‚Äîonly it so seldom is locked, as I told your father. Your father says it‚Äôs Lorry again; he‚Äôs made such a dreadful scene before Lewis.‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It‚Äôs not so much the fact of Lorry‚Äôs being out all night; but your father thinks he‚Äôs sold the car. And if he has we shall never see a penny of the money,‚Äù Mrs. Spear added, confusedly struggling to differentiate the causes of her distress, of which fear about the money was clearly by far the most potent.

H√©lo√Øse sat up in bed and gazed at her mother‚Äôs troubled countenance. ‚ÄúEven now,‚Äù she thought, ‚Äúher eyes are beautiful; and she doesn‚Äôt screw them up in the ugly way that I do.‚Äù Then she roused herself to reality. ‚ÄúWhat nonsense‚ÅÝ‚Äînobody stole the motor,‚Äù she said.
‚ÄúIt was Lorry, then? He‚Äôs told you‚ÅÝ‚Äî?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs told me nothing.‚Äù She paused, letting her imagination toy for a moment with the temptation to be silent; then she said: ‚ÄúThe motor‚Äôs a mile up the road, toward Thundertop. It broke down, and I left it there myself early this morning.‚Äù The idea of allowing Lorry to bear the brunt of the storm had lasted no more than the taking of breath between two words; but it had stirred in her an old residuum of self-disgust. Yet she did wish she had got the car safely under cover before all this fuss!
Her mother stood staring at her with astonished eyes. ‚ÄúYou, Halo? You had the car out in the middle of the night?‚Äù
‚ÄúYes. I had the car out. I went up to Thundertop to see the sunrise.‚Äù

‚ÄúWith Lewis, darling?‚Äù Mrs. Spear‚Äôs face brightened perceptibly; then it fell again. ‚ÄúBut no, he was there just now when your father scolded poor Lorry, and of course if he‚Äôd been with you‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù
‚ÄúHe wasn‚Äôt with me. He doesn‚Äôt know anything about it.‚Äù
‚ÄúHalo!‚Äù her mother moaned, ‚ÄúAnd you chose this time‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhen he‚Äôd just arrived!‚Äù She paused with a gesture of despair. ‚ÄúWho was with you?‚Äù

Halo, sliding out of bed, got her feet into her flopping Moroccan slippers, and began to gather up her sponge and towels preparatory to an advance to the bathroom. ‚ÄúOh, nobody in particular. Just that young boy from the Tracys‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù
Mrs. Spear gasped out her perplexity. ‚ÄúBoy from Tracys‚Äô? You don‚Äôt mean Upton?‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course not. How ridiculous! I mean the cousin from the West that they‚Äôve got staying there‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe boy who‚Äôs had typhoid fever. Didn‚Äôt I tell you about him? He‚Äôs rather extraordinary; full of talent, I believe; and starving to death for want of books, and of people he can talk to. I promised to spend an afternoon with him at the Willows, and let him browse in the library, and then I forgot all about it, and to make up for having chucked him I slipped out early this morning and ran him up to Thundertop to see the sunrise. It was glorious. And he read me some of his poems‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe means to be a poet. I do believe there‚Äôs something in him, Mother.‚Äù

While Mrs. Spear listened the expression of her beautiful eyes passed from anxiety to a sympathetic exaltation. As her daughter was aware, one could never speak to Mrs. Spear of genius without kindling in her an irrepressible ardour to encourage and direct it.

‚ÄúBut, my dear, how interesting! Why didn‚Äôt you tell me about him before? Couldn‚Äôt George Frenside do something to help him? Couldn‚Äôt he publish his things in The Hour? I do hope you asked him to come back to lunch?‚Äù

H√©lo√Øse laughed. Her mother‚Äôs enthusiasms always amused her. To hear of the presence, within inviting distance, of a young man of talent (talent was always genius to Mrs. Spear) was instantly to make her forget her family and financial cares, however pressing, and begin to wonder anxiously what the cook could scrape together for lunch‚ÅÝ‚Äîa sincere respect for good food being one of the anomalies of her oddly assorted character, and a succulent meal her instinctive homage to celebrity. ‚ÄúWhat time did you tell him to come‚ÅÝ‚Äîone or half-past? I believe Susan could still manage a cheese souffl√©‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut it‚Äôs nearly twelve now.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; I know it is‚ÅÝ‚Äîand you must give me a chance to take my bath. And how could I ask him to lunch? It wouldn‚Äôt be decent, when he‚Äôs staying with the Tracys, who, after all, are distant connections of ours, and whom we certainly don‚Äôt want to invite‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo we?‚Äù

Mrs. Spear, at this reminder, clasped her long expressive hands self-reproachfully. ‚ÄúOh, those poor Tracys! Yes, they are distant relations. But if Lorburn Tracy, who was a poor sort of man anyhow, and less than nobody on his father‚Äôs side, chose to marry the gardener‚Äôs daughter at the Willows, we could hardly be expected, could we‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ ? Not that that sort of thing really matters in the least. Why should it? Only‚ÅÝ‚Äîwell, perhaps we‚Äôve been snobbish about the Tracys, darling. Do you think we have? There‚Äôs nothing I hate so much as being snobbish. Do you think we ought to send Jacob down now with a note, and ask them all up to lunch with this young novelist you speak of? Not a novelist‚ÅÝ‚Äîa poet? What a head I‚Äôve got! My dear, I really believe we ought to. Is there any notepaper here? There so seldom is, in your room‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ but if you can find a scrap, do write a line to Mrs. Tracy, and say‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ say‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ well, put it as nicely as you can‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and say that Jacob is at their door, with the car, waiting to bring them up; oh, with the young man, of course! Remind me again of the young man‚Äôs name, my dear.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, if I said all that, Mother, part of it would hardly be true, as the car‚Äôs a mile or more up the Thundertop road at this minute, and refuses to budge.‚Äù H√©lo√Øse let her mother‚Äôs outcries evaporate, and continued quietly: ‚ÄúBesides, you know perfectly well that we can‚Äôt ask the Tracys to lunch. We never have, and why should we now? They‚Äôd hate it as much as we should. They‚Äôre plain working people, and they have nothing on earth to say to us, or we to them. Why should we suddenly pretend the contrary?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, Halo, how vulgar of you! I wonder how you can even think such things. All human beings ought to have things to say to each other, if only they meet on the broad basis of humanity and‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, the Tracys wouldn‚Äôt; how could they? They‚Äôve never heard of the broad basis of humanity. If we had them here with Lewis and George Frenside, or any of our other friends, what on earth should we find to say to each other? They talk another language, and it can‚Äôt be helped. But this boy is different, and I‚Äôve promised to take him to the Willows to go through the books, and to bring George down to see him there some day. So let‚Äôs let it go at that.‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù And H√©lo√Øse, followed by her mother‚Äôs reproaches and ejaculations, made a dive for the door and dashed down the passage to the bathroom.

When she descended the worn shallow steps of the old staircase the lunch gong was sounding, and Mr. Spear was pacing the hall in a state of repressed excitement. Halo and Lorry, as children, had found in a popular natural history book a striking picture of a bristling caterpillar sitting upright on its tail, with the caption: ‚ÄúThe male Puss-Moth when irritated after a full meal.‚Äù They instantly christened their progenitor the Puss-Moth, but modified the legend by explaining to strangers that he was more often irritated before a full meal than after‚ÅÝ‚Äîespecially when kept waiting for it. Today Mr. Spear was unusually perpendicular and bristling, and his small, rather too carefully modeled features were positively grimacing with anger. To his daughter long habit made the sight more comic than impressive; but she was vexed that the question of the motor had to be dealt with.
‚ÄúHalo, I suppose you know the car was taken out of the garage last night, without the door lock‚Äôs being broken, and that it has not since been seen? Your mother tells me‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù
Halo nodded. ‚ÄúMother knows. I‚Äôve explained.‚Äù
‚ÄúExplained! I don‚Äôt know what you call‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúHullo, Lewis‚ÅÝ‚Äîgood morning.‚Äù Halo interrupted her father‚Äôs diatribe to greet Lewis Tarrant, who came lounging in from the verandah, followed by George Frenside munching his eternal cigar. ‚ÄúGood morning, Frenny‚ÅÝ‚Äîif it‚Äôs still morning? Are you all coming to assist at my execution?‚Äù she laughed.
‚ÄúWe want to hear the argument for the defence,‚Äù Frenside retorted, in his deep voice with the queer crack in it.
‚ÄúIsn‚Äôt any. I plead guilty. I had the car out in the small hours, and busted her coming down the Thundertop road. I had to get home on my own feet, and that‚Äôs all.‚Äù
‚ÄúAll‚ÅÝ‚Äîall? You say it was you who was out in the car in the middle of the night?‚Äù Mr. Spear swung round on young Tarrant. ‚ÄúLewis‚ÅÝ‚Äîwas she?‚Äù
Lewis Tarrant‚Äôs fair complexion, which so curiously matched his very fair and very clear light gray eyes, grew sallow with surprise and embarrassment.
‚ÄúI‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhy, of course, sir, if she says so,‚Äù he stammered.
‚Äú ‚ÄòSays so‚Äô? Were you with her‚ÅÝ‚Äîor weren‚Äôt you? If you weren‚Äôt she‚Äôs only shielding her brother for the hundredth time; and God knows where the car‚Äôs gone to, by this time.‚Äù

‚ÄúSend Jacob up the road to see,‚Äù H√©lo√Øse interrupted impatiently. She had meant to carry the thing off with a light hand, laughing at them all, turning the affair into a good story to be served up to future guests; they were great, at Eaglewood, on collecting good stories for social purposes. But she had been put off her balance by the expression of Lewis Tarrant‚Äôs face, the intonation of his voice. Evidently he too supposed she was lying to shield her brother, and like the gentleman he was he was going to shield her; and the business was not in the least to his liking. (‚ÄúWell, he knows what we‚Äôre like; why does he keep on coming here?‚Äù she thought, almost putting the question aloud to him.) She swung away from both men with one of her quick rebellious movements.

‚ÄúAnd now, for goodness‚Äô sake, Father, don‚Äôt go on keeping everybody waiting for lunch. Their interest in this affair is purely one of politeness. They don‚Äôt care a hang who had the car out last night, and they know it‚Äôs not the first time she‚Äôs broken down, especially when I‚Äôve been driving her.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo‚ÅÝ‚Äîas to that, sir, Halo‚Äôs right, you know,‚Äù Tarrant corroborated laughingly, and at the same moment Mrs. Spear appeared, her face brightening as she caught the echo of the laugh. But her exhilaration did not last. ‚ÄúI do think, Halo, we ought to have invited the Tracys here long ago,‚Äù she began, the lines forming again about her eyes and mouth.

‚ÄúThe Tracys‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe Tracys‚ÅÝ‚Äîwho on earth do you mean by the Tracys?‚Äù Mr. Spear broke in, glad of a new vent for his irritation. ‚ÄúNot Tom Lorburn‚Äôs caretakers at the Willows, I take it?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy, you know, Harold, the Tracys really are related to Tom Lorburn and me, and I‚Äôm afraid we‚Äôve been dreadfully distant to them, never going near them except to see whether they‚Äôd looked after the Willows properly‚ÅÝ‚Äîand now Halo says they have a young cousin from the West staying there‚ÅÝ‚Äîa painter, no, I mean a poet, who‚Äôs really a genius. And so I thought‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

Mrs. Spear‚Äôs confused explanation was interrupted by the convulsive splutter and angry pause of a motor outside of the house. So familiar were the sounds to all present that H√©lo√Øse merely said with a shrug: ‚ÄúWhat did I tell you?‚Äù and turned toward the dining room door, in the hope that the sight of food would cut short the investigation of her nocturnal trip.

‚ÄúWell, Jacob?‚Äù Mr. Spear exclaimed, pausing halfway across the hall; and his daughter, turning, met the reproachful gaze of the family factotum, who stood in the doorway mopping the moisture from his perpetually puzzled wrinkles.

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôve got her,‚Äù Jacob said. He glanced about him somewhat apprehensively, and then, fixing his eyes again on H√©lo√Øse: ‚ÄúI found a fellow sitting in her. He said he‚Äôd lost his way and gone to sleep. He was going to cut and run, but I told him he‚Äôd have to come here with me and tell you folks what he was doing in the car anyhow.‚Äù
Jacob fell back, and over his shoulder H√©lo√Øse caught sight of a slim boyish figure with white face and rumpled hair, and deep eyes still bewildered with sleep.
‚ÄúIs this Eaglewood?‚Äù Vance Weston asked of the assembled company; then he saw Miss Spear behind the others, and his pallor turned to crimson.
Halo came forward. ‚ÄúVance‚ÅÝ‚Äîhow wonderful. Mother was just asking why I hadn‚Äôt brought you back to lunch; and here you are!‚Äù

He looked at her as if only half understanding. ‚ÄúI couldn‚Äôt find my way home, so I came back up the road, and when I saw the car was still there I got into it to wait until somebody came along‚ÅÝ‚Äîand I guess I must have fallen asleep.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, that‚Äôs all right; it‚Äôs even providential. You‚Äôve saved Jacob the trouble of going all the way down the hill to get you. Mother, Father, this is Vance Weston, who‚Äôs staying at Paul‚Äôs Landing with the Tracys. Mother, can‚Äôt we have something to eat? You‚Äôve no idea how inhumanly hungry sunrises make people‚ÅÝ‚Äîdon‚Äôt they, Vance? Oh, and this is Mr. Frenside, whom I‚Äôve told you about: who writes for The Hour. And this is Mr. Lewis Tarrant‚ÅÝ‚Äîand here‚Äôs my brother Lorburn. I suppose you‚Äôre our cousin, too, aren‚Äôt you, Vance? Lorry, this is our new cousin, Vance Weston.‚Äù

As she performed this rapid ceremony Halo‚Äôs eyes dwelt a moment longer on Lewis Tarrant‚Äôs face than on the others. Ah, he was taking his dose now‚ÅÝ‚Äîa nasty brew! ‚ÄúShielding‚Äù her again; much she cared about being shielded! He would know now that she really had been out in the car in the night, that this unknown boy had been with her, that she didn‚Äôt care a fig who knew it, and that the escapade had taken place at the very moment when he, Lewis Tarrant, had come to Eaglewood for the weekend, on her express promise that she would tell him definitely, before the end of his visit, if she were going to marry him or not.‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

Lorry Spear was the first to break the silence which had followed on young Weston‚Äôs entrance. ‚ÄúI see that our new cousin has done us the doubtful service of preventing that rotten old car from being stolen. At least we might have collected the insurance on her.‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Glad to see you all the same, Weston; don‚Äôt bear you the least grudge.‚Äù He held out his hand to the increasingly bewildered Vance.

‚ÄúHow absurd, Lorry‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ as if anybody had really thought‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù Mrs. Spear broke in, the cloud lifting from her brow as she saw that her son was helping to carry the thing off (he didn‚Äôt always; but when he did he was masterly).

‚ÄúAnd all this time, my dear Vance,‚Äù Mrs. Spear continued turning her beautiful eyes on her guest, ‚Äúyou must be wondering what we‚Äôre all talking about, and why lunch is so late. But it‚Äôs providential, as Halo says; for we shouldn‚Äôt have had the pleasure of having you with us if that stupid old motor hadn‚Äôt broken down. Now come into the dining room, my dear boy, this way. I‚Äôm going to put you next to Mr. Frenside, our great critic, whom you know by reputation‚ÅÝ‚Äîof course you read The Hour? George, this is Halo‚Äôs friend, the young novelist‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ no, poet‚ÅÝ ‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ poet, isn‚Äôt it, Vance? You happy being!‚Äù Mrs. Spear laid her urgent hand on his shoulder and drew him toward the luncheon table.
